nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, fast-flowing blackwater streams and rivers with rocky substrates, submerged driftwood, and leaf litter; often found sheltering in crevices and under logs in low-light conditions

decorPreferences: driftwood (essential — dietary fiber and grazing surface)
cave-like hiding spots (terracotta tubes, coconut hides, PVC pipe)
smooth or fine-grain substrate (sand or small gravel)
flat rocks and slate for grazing
dense planting optional (Java fern, Anubias, mosses tolerate shading)
moderate to strong current with high oxygenation

diet.notes: Primarily an algae-grazer and wood-rasper; driftwood should always be present as a dietary fiber source. Diet should emphasize algae wafers, spirulina tabs, blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, spinach, peas), and raw cucumber slices left overnight. Also accepts high-quality sinking pellets and repashy gel foods. Supplement with small amounts of protein (bloodworm, brine shrimp) 1–2× weekly — the species is herbivore-leaning but consumes biofilm, aufwuchs, and occasional invertebrates in the wild. Unlike carnivore-leaning loricariids such as Hypancistrus, Ancistrus genuinely rasps algae and is a functional algae-control species.

avoidWith: large aggressive cichlids (oscars, flowerhorn) — pleco suckers may latch on fish
discus and other large slow-moving flat-bodied fish (sucker harassment risk)
very small nano fish where pleco size creates intimidation
salt-sensitive tankmates if salt additive is used

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Ancistrus dolichopterus; confirm availability at review — FishBase image URLs can rotate.
Aquarium specimen at Wuppertal Zoo; clear lateral view showing bristle development on male snout.

summary: The Bushy Nose (Bristlenose) Pleco is a hardy, algae-grazing armored catfish reaching 12–15 cm, renowned for the elaborate branched fleshy bristles that develop on the snout of mature males. One of the most beginner-friendly plecos and a reliable algae-control fish for community tanks from 110 L upward.
careNotes:
The Bushy Nose Pleco is among the most popular and practical plecos for home aquaria, largely because it stays compact (10–15 cm) compared to the common pleco (Pterygoplichthys spp., which can exceed 45 cm). The most distinctive feature is the male's elaborate fleshy, branched bristles ('bushy nose') that develop on the snout tip and around the upper lip — females have small, shorter bristles restricted to the snout margin, while juveniles of both sexes are initially indistinguishable.

Driftwood must be present in the tank: Ancistrus rasps wood constantly, gaining both dietary fiber (essential for gut health) and biofilm. Zucchini slices, cucumber rounds, and algae wafers placed near the substrate at night are eagerly accepted. Despite being sold partly as an algae-control species, the pleco still needs supplemental feeding — it will not subsist on tank glass alone.

Cave territories are critical: males aggressively defend hollows from rival males and from other cave-spawning fish. Provide at least one cave per male (terracotta tubes and coconut hides work well). Males in particular are territorial with conspecifics — a single male in a community tank is the standard recommendation; two males require a large tank (200+ L) with multiple well-separated caves.

The species is sold under the trade name 'Bristlenose' or 'Bushy Nose,' but the Ancistrus genus is a species complex. Wild-caught fish labeled A. dolichopterus are often A. cirrhosus or undescribed hybrids. The true A. dolichopterus is a Rio Negro blackwater fish (pH 4.5–6.5, very soft), but virtually all aquarium specimens are multi-generational captive-bred and tolerate a wide pH range (5.5–7.5) and moderate hardness without issue. Soft, slightly acidic water is preferred but not required for captive-bred stock.

Water movement should be moderate to strong with high oxygenation — the species originates in well-aerated stream environments and is sensitive to low dissolved oxygen and poor water quality. Weekly water changes of 25–30% are important; Loricariids are susceptible to bacterial and fungal infections in stagnant or dirty water. Avoid copper-based medications (standard caution for all scaleless/armored catfish).

See also: [common-pleco] (Pterygoplichthys pardalis) — grows to 45+ cm, unsuitable for most home tanks; [clown-pleco] (Panaqolus maccus, L104) — similar cave-dweller but more wood-dependent; [zebra-pleco] (Hypancistrus zebra, L046) — carnivore, specialist care, CITES-restricted.
breedingNotes:
One of the most readily bred plecos in captivity — a significant advantage for hobbyists wanting to propagate their own stock. Males select and defend a cave, enticing females inside; spawning occurs within the cave and the male provides exclusive parental care, fanning and guarding the bright orange-yellow egg clutch (30–100+ eggs) for 4–7 days until hatching. Fry cling to the cave wall and absorb their yolk sac over a further 2–4 days before becoming free-swimming. The male continues guarding fry briefly after they become free-swimming.

To trigger spawning: condition both sexes with varied vegetable-rich diet; perform a slightly larger, slightly cooler water change to simulate rainy-season inflow (drop 1–2°C); ensure a suitable cave that is just large enough for the male to barely turn around in. Separating the spawning cave into a dedicated breeding tank improves fry survival. Fry are herbivorous from the start and accept algae wafers, spirulina powder, and finely grated zucchini. Growth is relatively slow — juveniles reach 3–4 cm in approximately 3 months at 26°C.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Ancistrus dolichopterus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, temperature range (23–29°C), pH (5.5–7.5), hardness (36–268 ppm / 2–15 dH), native range (Rio Negro, Brazil), biotope (blackwater, fast-flowing), diet (algae, wood, vegetable matter), cave-breeding behaviour, and species-complex taxonomy note distinguishing wild A. dolichopterus from captive-bred Ancistrus strains.

additional.name: FishBase — Ancistrus dolichopterus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 14.0 cm SL), taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), native range (Rio Negro drainage, Brazil), temperature (22–28°C), trophic level (herbivore/detritivore). Confirmed IUCN Least Concern. Species listed under multiple synonyms consistent with Ancistrus species-complex instability.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Bristlenose Pleco Care Guide
additional.notes: Used for trade-level care guidance: tank size (30 gal / ~114 L minimum), temperature (60–80°F / 15.5–26.7°C — note wide range, captive-bred tolerance), diet emphasis on vegetables and driftwood, beginner suitability, and sexing by bristle morphology. Note: Aquarium Co-Op uses 'Bristlenose Pleco' as a broad trade grouping rather than strictly A. dolichopterus.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Ancistrus species (Bristlenose catfish)
additional.notes: Supplemented cave-breeding behaviour details (male parental care, egg clutch size 30–100+, incubation 4–7 days), conspecific male aggression, driftwood dietary requirement, and species-complex taxonomic instability notes. Confirmed pH range 5.5–7.5 and soft-to-moderate hardness preference.

additional.name: PlanetCatfish — Ancistrus dolichopterus (L183)
additional.notes: L-number trade reference (L183). Confirmed Rio Negro blackwater origin for wild-type, adult size, cave spawning behaviour, and notes on captive-bred versus wild stock pH tolerance. Useful secondary corroboration for loricariid-specific details not covered by mainstream fishkeeping sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawner; male guards and fans clutch of 30–100+ eggs for 4–7 days, then fry for a further few days. Provide a snug cave (barely fits male) and condition with vegetable-rich diet. One of the most readily bred plecos in captivity — spawns reliably in a well-maintained community tank with caves present.
